title: Executive Order 9672—Establishing the National Wage Stabilization Board and Terminating the National War Labor Board

flags.justification: A six-member tripartite board (two public, two employee, two employer) that is explicitly 'independent with respect to its policies, decisions, and control of personnel' (Sec. 1d) — the same balanced design already found in its predecessor, EO 9017.

Clear quorum rules and a narrower scope than its predecessor (wage stabilization specifically); Sec. 3 explicitly returns unresolved dispute cases to the parties rather than letting the new Board unilaterally decide them, except for specifically enumerated continuing functions — a real due-process safeguard.
The Board's powers are anchored to specific existing statutes (Emergency Price Control Act, Stabilization Act) and their associated orders and regulations.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
No explicit termination date is set for the Board itself, though it is tied to underlying wartime economic-stabilization statutes that would themselves eventually expire.
A designed succession from NWLB, explicitly preserving continuity of 'present effective rules, regulations, procedures, and orders' (Sec. 5) rather than removing oversight.
The balanced tripartite structure and explicit independence clause provide real structural checks.

This narrows its predecessor's broad wartime labor-dispute mandate down to a more focused wage-stabilization function as the economy demobilizes — an appropriately scoped-down successor, not an expansion of authority.

Sec. 4 formally transfers NWLB's personnel, records, and funds in a clean succession, and Sec. 2 explicitly preserves the separate Wage Adjustment Board's existing functions — deliberate avoidance of overlap.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/20 = 5.0%. A well-designed successor institution to EO 9017's National War Labor Board, appropriately narrowed in scope for the postwar transition.
